category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Cold with NO sweat (powerful diaphoretic) · 2) Cough, wheezing, asthma · 3) Wind-water edema with urinary difficulty
contraindication: May cause sweating and/or hypertension; caution with Qi/Yin deficiency
boardPearl: "Summertime Ma Huang" = Xiang Ru. Dui Yao with Gui Zhi for severe Wind-Cold. Ma Huang enters Qi Level; Gui Zhi enters Blood Level. Honey-prepared (Zhi Ma Huang) = better for cough/asthma; Raw = stronger diaphoretic.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Cold WITH sweat (deficiency type) · 2) Warms the middle, improves blood circulation · 3) Harmonizes Ying and Wei; Bi Syndrome
contraindication: Use only small doses in pregnancy
boardPearl: Commonly used for Bi Syndrome. Dui Yao with Ma Huang for severe Wind-Cold. Unblocks Yang Qi of the chest (chest bi syndrome).

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Cold with nausea/vomiting · 2) Seafood poisoning · 3) Calms the fetus / morning sickness
contraindication: Dispersing nature — caution with Qi deficiency
boardPearl: WARE herb for nausea, vomiting, poor appetite AND calming restless fetus. Also treats seafood poisoning (along with Sheng Jiang).

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Skin rashes, allergic reactions · 2) Vents early-stage measles · 3) Headache and fever
boardPearl: Used for BOTH Wind-Cold AND Wind-Heat. Stops bleeding when charred.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Cold with dampness · 2) Relieves pain · 3) Promotes diuresis
boardPearl: Especially for Wind-Cold accompanied by dampness. Called 'moisturizer among wind herbs' — relatively gentle.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Damp with headaches/body aches · 2) Occipital (Tai Yang) headache · 3) Promotes sweating, relieves pain
boardPearl: Guides to Tai Yang and Du. Occipital headache. Dui Yao with Du Huo — one moderate (Du Huo), one powerful (Qiang Huo).

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Cold with digestive disorders · 2) Summer dampness and edema · 3) Promotes sweating, relieves nausea
boardPearl: "Summertime Ma Huang" — treats summer dampness and edema with urinary difficulty.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Vertex headache · 2) Neck pain from Wind-Cold · 3) Promotes sweating, relieves pain
boardPearl: Guides to Tai Yang and Du. Key herb for VERTEX headache.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Sinus congestion · 2) Frontal / Yang Ming headache · 3) Promotes healing of skin conditions
boardPearl: Frontal (Yang Ming) headache. Key herb to unblock the nose (along with Cang Er Zi).

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Cold with headache/body aches · 2) Opens orifices, transforms thin mucus · 3) Toothache
contraindication: Toxic in large doses; caution with Yin deficiency/sweating
boardPearl: Opens the orifices. Treats toothache. Transforms thin mucus.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Colds, coughs, digestive disorders · 2) Nausea and vomiting · 3) Seafood poisoning; reduces toxicity of Ban Xia
boardPearl: Reduces toxicity of Ban Xia. Early-stage Wind-Cold tea with Cong Bai.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Very early stage Wind-Cold · 2) Promotes sweating, relieves congestion · 3) Disperses clumps
boardPearl: Tea with Sheng Jiang for very early stage Wind-Cold.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Sinusitis and nasal congestion · 2) Headache and toothache · 3) Itching, skin conditions
boardPearl: Itching + rashes AND opens nasal passages. Key herb to unblock the nose (with Bai Zhi).

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Warm Acrid
indications: 1) Sinus congestion from Wind-Cold · 2) Headache · 3) Nasal discharge
boardPearl: Must be placed in cheesecloth/gauze bag prior to decocting.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Cool Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Heat, Upper Jiao · 2) Constrained Liver Qi (with Chai Hu & Bai Shao) · 3) Clears turbid filth, helps Qi flow
boardPearl: Add in last 5 minutes of decocting. Used with Chai Hu & Bai Shao for constrained LV Qi with emotional instability.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Cool Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Heat with sore throat · 2) Skin disorders, relieves itching · 3) Unblocks bowel, promotes urination
boardPearl: Slippery nature — promotes movement of both stool and urine. Benefits throat.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Cool Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Heat with rashes/hives · 2) Relieves itching, promotes sweating · 3) Soothes throat
boardPearl: The SALTY herb in the CARE category.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Cool Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Heat, clears LV · 2) Dry cough · 3) Benefits eyes
boardPearl: Dui Yao with Ju Hua for LV, eyes, LU, and cough.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Cool Acrid
indications: 1) Eye disorders (#1 — conjunctivitis, cataracts) · 2) Headaches · 3) Clears heat, dispels Wind-Heat
boardPearl: #1 for Wind-Heat affecting the eyes. Dui Yao with Sang Ye.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Cool Acrid
indications: 1) Temporal (Shao Yang) excess headache · 2) Wind-Heat affecting face and eyes · 3) Bi Syndrome
boardPearl: Only CARE herb that treats Bi Syndrome. Shao Yang (temporal) excess headache.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Cool Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Heat OR Wind-Cold exterior release · 2) Relieves irritability · 3) Fevers and digestive disorders
boardPearl: Releases exterior in BOTH hot or cold syndrome. Does not injure Yin.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Cool Acrid
indications: 1) Releases the muscle layer; neck/shoulder tension · 2) Generates/promotes fluids · 3) Raises Yang; vents measles
boardPearl: Raises Yang. Releases muscle layer. Treats symptoms of hypertension. Guides other herbs upward.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Cool Acrid
indications: 1) Liver Qi stagnation · 2) Harmonizes Shao Yang (alternating fever/chills) · 3) Raises Yang
contraindication: Caution with LV wind rising, Yin deficiency
boardPearl: Harmonizes Shao Yang. Raises Yang. Checks malarial disorders (along with Qing Hao).

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Cool Acrid
indications: 1) #1 for measles · 2) Yang Ming (frontal) headaches · 3) Raises Yang; Dai (Belt) Channel spasms
boardPearl: #1 for measles. Raises Yang. Indicated for Dai (Belt/Girdling) Channel spasms.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Cool Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Heat affecting eyes · 2) Superficial visual obstructions · 3) Promotes urination
boardPearl: Best for removing membranes from conjunctiva in superficial visual obstruction.

category: Herbs that Release the Exterior — Cool Acrid
indications: 1) Wind-Heat · 2) Eye disorders · 3) Promotes urination
contraindication: Strongly diaphoretic — caution with sweating deficiency
boardPearl: Strongly diaphoretic.

indications: 1) #1 to clear internal heat at QI LEVEL · 2) High fever, thirst, cough (4 Bigs) · 3) Burns, sores
boardPearl: Coldest, most important to clear internal heat at Qi Level. Mineral. Dui Yao with Zhi Mu for "4 Bigs" — protects fluids and yin. Must be decocted first (30 min before other herbs) since it's a mineral.

indications: 1) Yin deficiency-type fever (deficiency heat) · 2) Fever, irritability, insomnia, night sweats · 3) Moistening — ameliorates dryness of warming substances
boardPearl: Clears BOTH repletion AND vacuity heat. More for deficiency heat. Moistening. Dui Yao with Shi Gao. Dui Yao with Huang Bai for KD.

indications: 1) Clears Damp-Heat in ALL 3 JIAOS · 2) Directs Damp-Heat down through urine · 3) Fever, headache, red eyes, jaundice
boardPearl: All 3 Jiaos. Cools blood when charred. Must be crushed prior to decoction.

indications: 1) Damp-Heat in bladder with painful urination · 2) Promotes urination · 3) Treats associated irritability
boardPearl: Best for fever, irritability, thirst AND dysuria.

indications: 1) Clears LV and eyes · 2) Nodules (especially inguinal area) · 3) Sore throat, gum inflammation
boardPearl: Drains LV fire, dissipates nodules/goiter, benefits eyes. Key herb for scrofula and phlegm-fire nodules.

indications: 1) Summer heatstroke · 2) Generates fluids · 3) Promotes diuresis, expels jaundice
boardPearl: Watermelon. Summer heatstroke herb. Generates fluids.

indications: 1) Clears heat, generates body fluids · 2) Cough, thirst, dry mouth · 3) Breast abscess
contraindication: SP/ST cold deficiency; contra with Fu Zi
boardPearl: Breast abscess. Generates fluids for LU and ST.

indications: 1) Clears heat, relieves thirst · 2) Treats heat when Yin has been injured · 3) Nausea from rebellion of Stomach Qi
boardPearl: Excellent for treating nausea due to rebellion of Stomach Qi.

indications: 1) Eyes (clears LV) · 2) Constipation due to heat accumulation · 3) Headache and dizziness
boardPearl: Eyes — clears LV. Moves the bowels.

indications: 1) Cools blood, reduces high fever · 2) Calms mind — delirium, coma, convulsions · 3) Arrests tremors, helps rashes
boardPearl: #1 for clearing fire from the HT and cooling the blood. Water buffalo horn — replacement for Xi Jiao (Rhinoceros horn).

indications: 1) Nourishes Yin, cools blood (whole body) · 2) High fever, irritability, insomnia · 3) Constipation from heat/dryness
contraindication: May cause dampness; caution with loose stools
boardPearl: Cloying. Nourishes Yin, cools blood whole body. Best for febrile disease when heat enters the Ying stage. Note: Shui Niu Jiao (not Sheng Di) is the direct replacement for Xi Jiao.

indications: 1) Clears heat, cools blood, nourishes Yin (excess) · 2) Throat pain and scrofula · 3) Fever, dry mouth, constipation
boardPearl: Excess type. Throat pain and scrofula. Nourishes Yin and generates fluids.

indications: 1) Cools blood AND invigorates blood · 2) Reduces inflammation · 3) Clears deficiency heat
contraindication: Caution in pregnancy; caution with excess menstrual bleeding
boardPearl: Unique: Cools blood AND invigorates blood. Treats both excess and Xu. Clears deficiency heat.

indications: 1) Cools blood, promotes skin healing · 2) Vents rashes · 3) Sore throat, fever
boardPearl: Only function in category is to vent rashes. Topical. Best for severe Heat-Toxin with inability of dark-purple rashes to erupt.

indications: 1) Clears heat/dries damp — Upper Jiao (LU) · 2) Sedates LV Yang · 3) Quiets the fetus
boardPearl: Upper Jiao — LU. First choice for LU heat causing cough. Stops bleeding when charred. Quiets fetus.

indications: 1) #1 for diarrhea, dysentery, vomiting (Damp-Heat) · 2) Clears heat from ST on both ends · 3) Topical for gums; stops bleeding
boardPearl: #1 herb for Damp-Heat diarrhea, dysentery, and vomiting. Clears HT fire. Topical for gums. Middle Jiao specialist. Note: the "4 Bigs" belong to Shi Gao (Qi Level).

indications: 1) Clears Damp-Heat in LOWER JIAO · 2) Hot Leg Qi · 3) Clears both excess and deficient heat (Dui Yao with Zhi Mu for KD)
boardPearl: Lower Jiao specialist. Dui Yao with Zhi Mu for KD — clears both excess and deficient heat.

indications: 1) Drains Damp-Heat from LV/GB channels · 2) Vaginal discharge and itching from Damp-Heat · 3) Jaundice, eye infections, anger
boardPearl: BEST to clear heat, dry damp, and purge LV fire. Treats vaginal discharge and itching due to Damp-Heat.

indications: 1) Chronic itching, skin diseases · 2) Expels wind, kills parasites · 3) Genital sores
boardPearl: Dispels wind and kills parasites. Skin disease and genital sores.

indications: 1) Eyes · 2) Binds intestines (diarrhea) · 3) Wind-Damp Bi
boardPearl: Eyes. Binds intestines. Wind-Damp-Bi.

indications: 1) Clears toxic heat — breast, throat, eye · 2) External Wind-Heat · 3) Venting and dispersing
boardPearl: Dui Yao with Lian Qiao to clear Wind-Heat from Upper Jiao. Breast, throat, eye.

indications: 1) Clears heat and toxins — skin infections, tonsillitis · 2) Disperses heat from constraint · 3) Removes toxins, dissipates clumps, reduces abscesses
boardPearl: Dui Yao with Jin Yin Hua. Dispersing heat from constraint. Best to clear heat in HT and disperse stagnation.

indications: 1) Severe sore throat · 2) Acute pharyngitis, tonsillitis, carbuncles · 3) Measles
boardPearl: Colder than Ban Lan Gen (from same plant).

indications: 1) Common cold, flu, viral infections · 2) Red, swollen throat — tonsillitis, scarlet fever, mumps · 3) Prevents viral epidemics
boardPearl: Not as cold as Da Qing Ye. Can be used to prevent viral epidemics.

indications: 1) Breast abscesses · 2) Clears heat/toxins from liver — hepatitis, jaundice · 3) Promotes lactation
boardPearl: Dandelion. Breast abscesses. Promotes lactation. Steam for eyes (LV heat).

indications: 1) Skin infections, boils, abscesses · 2) Red swollen eyes, sore throat · 3) Snake bite; mumps
boardPearl: Red swollen eyes, very sore throat, pain in ears, mumps, snake bite.

indications: 1) Damp-Heat in blood — itchy skin lesions · 2) Jaundice · 3) Vaginal discharge
boardPearl: Damp-Heat in blood leading to itchy skin lesions.

indications: 1) Strong antimicrobial — respiratory infections · 2) Digestive system infections · 3) Anywhere in the body
boardPearl: Strong antimicrobial. Anywhere in the body.

indications: 1) Intestinal abscess from Damp-Heat · 2) Drains damp through urination · 3) Variety of cancers (anti-tumor)
boardPearl: Anti-tumor effect — used for a variety of cancers. Named for leaves resembling snake's tongue.

indications: 1) Dysentery due to Damp-Heat · 2) Respiratory infections · 3) Digestive infections
boardPearl: Dysentery due to Damp-Heat. Used with Ma Chi Xian for dysentery.

indications: 1) Dysentery (with Bai Tou Weng) · 2) Severe tenesmus (slippery) · 3) Stops bleeding in urine; gynecology
boardPearl: "Common Purslane." Slippery — used for severe tenesmus.

indications: 1) Diarrhea from SP Qi deficiency (esp. after summerheat) · 2) Bleeding in Lower Jiao · 3) Raises clear Yang of Spleen
boardPearl: Important for clearing summerheat. Raises clear Yang of Spleen. Stops bleeding.

indications: 1) Heat-related conditions — fever, thirst, irritability · 2) Summerheat · 3) Antidote to Zhi Fu Zi and Ba Dou
boardPearl: Mung beans. Antidote to Zhi Fu Zi and Ba Dou. Summerheat.

indications: 1) Skin infections, eczema · 2) Intestinal abscess — dispels blood stasis · 3) Hot skin lesions (Damp-Heat)
boardPearl: Important for treating Damp-Heat with hot skin lesions.

indications: 1) Severe sore throat, swollen teeth/gums · 2) Cancer of lung and throat · 3) Respiratory/digestive infections
boardPearl: Severe sore throat pain and throat/lung cancers.

indications: 1) Drains Damp for joint pain · 2) Late-stage syphilis (muscle spasm) · 3) Hot skin lesions
boardPearl: Late-stage muscle spasm associated with syphilis. Treats skin lesions and Damp-Heat.

indications: 1) Severe sore throat · 2) Transforming phlegm, wheezing · 3) Inflammatory skin conditions
boardPearl: Benefits throat and dissolves Hot-Phlegm. Treats wheezing.

indications: 1) Clears deficiency heat without injuring Qi, Blood, or Yin · 2) Malaria · 3) Summerheat
contraindication: Weak digestion and diarrhea; SP/ST deficiency cold
boardPearl: "Sweet Annie." Clears deficiency heat and treats malaria. Also addresses summerheat but primary fame is for clearing Yin-level heat without injuring Qi/Blood/Yin. Checks malarial disorders (with Chai Hu).

indications: 1) Steaming bone disorder with sweating · 2) Nourishes Yin/Blood while clearing heat · 3) Cools LU; toothache
boardPearl: Wolfberry bark. Clears heat from both excess and deficiency in blood. Steaming bone disorder. Cools LU.

indications: 1) Yin/fluid deficiency heat (esp. upper body) · 2) Sore throat and cough · 3) Nutritive and Blood level heat
boardPearl: Swallowwort root. Nutritive and Blood level. Does NOT moisten the intestine (differentiates from others).

indications: 1) Clears deficiency heat — fever, irritability, thirst · 2) Childhood nutritional impairment · 3) Steaming bone disorder
boardPearl: Starwort root. Especially effective for steaming bone disorder and childhood nutritional impairment.

indications: 1) Most important herb for excess heat accumulation in intestines · 2) Blood stasis, amenorrhea, traumatic injury · 3) Cools blood, stops bleeding without causing stasis
boardPearl: Dui Yao with Mang Xiao (emphasis on draining heat). Stops bleeding without causing stasis. Wine-prepared = directed to upper body; charred = enhanced hemostatic effect.

indications: 1) Softens hard stools · 2) Relieves constipation · 3) Promotes lactation for breast abscess
contraindication: Pregnancy; nursing (reduces lactation at high dose)
boardPearl: Mirabilite/Glauber's Salt. Dui Yao with Da Huang (emphasis on softening hardness). Softens hard stools.

indications: 1) Habitual constipation · 2) Stronger bowel-moving effect than Da Huang · 3) Constipation in weak/elderly
boardPearl: Senna Leaf. Moist in nature. Stronger bowel-moving effect than Da Huang.

indications: 1) Constipation with HT/LV fire · 2) Cools Liver · 3) Kills parasites
boardPearl: Purgative for patients with Heart and Liver fire. Kills parasites.

indications: 1) Dry constipation — enriches Yin, moistens intestines · 2) Post-surgery/postpartum/elderly constipation · 3) Hemorrhoids, habitual constipation
boardPearl: Moist laxative. Sweet and neutral. For deficiency constipation in elderly/postpartum.

indications: 1) Deficiency constipation · 2) Constipation from Qi stagnation · 3) Lubricates intestines, promotes BM
boardPearl: Treats constipation from Qi stagnation (differentiates from Huo Ma Ren which does not).

indications: 1) Edema with distention/fullness · 2) Clears Damp-Heat in Qi level of all 3 Jiaos · 3) Cough, rebellious Qi, wheezing with constipation
boardPearl: Morning Glory seed. Deals with all 4 pathogenic fluids: phlegm, thin mucus, dampness, water.

indications: 1) Ascites (excess type) · 2) Severe constipation · 3) Eliminates excess fluids
contraindication: VERY TOXIC — short-term use only; contra in pregnancy
boardPearl: Very toxic. For ascites. Violent cathartic — causes water to pass anally.

indications: 1) Violent purgative for cold accumulation constipation · 2) Stubborn phlegm accumulation · 3) Ascites (schistosomiasis)
contraindication: EXTREMELY TOXIC — very small dose (0.1–0.3g/day); can cause fatality
boardPearl: "Herbal equivalent of a battering ram." Acrid, hot, toxic. Pills/capsules only. Antidote: Lu Dou.

indications: 1) Strengthens Spleen, drains dampness (#1 in category) · 2) Promotes Middle Jiao harmony · 3) Superficial edema, urinary difficulty, diarrhea in children
boardPearl: Most commonly used herb in the Drain Damp category. Fungus.

indications: 1) Drains dampness, promotes urination · 2) Mobilizes exterior to reduce subcutaneous edema · 3) More diuretic than Fu Ling
boardPearl: Outer skin of Fu Ling. More diuretic than Fu Ling. Mobilizes exterior for subcutaneous edema.

indications: 1) Calms the Heart, quiets the Spirit · 2) Promotes urination, drains dampness · 3) Insomnia, anxiety
boardPearl: Fungus around the root. PRIMARY action is calming the spirit.

indications: 1) Drains damp, clears heat · 2) Moves water · 3) Strengthens SP, resolves dampness
boardPearl: Red in color. For moving water it "cannot be beat."

indications: 1) Drains dampness (Lower Jiao) · 2) Promotes urination · 3) Unblocks painful urinary dribbling
boardPearl: Lower Jiao specialist for draining dampness.

indications: 1) Drains dampness, strengthens SP · 2) Eliminates painful obstruction (Bi Syndrome) · 3) Harmonizes middle burner, promotes digestion
boardPearl: Job's Tears. Facilitates resolution of dampness, strengthens SP, eliminates painful obstruction.

indications: 1) Drains dampness, promotes urination · 2) Unblocks painful urinary dribbling · 3) Clears eyes, benefits vision
boardPearl: Must be placed in cheesecloth/gauze prior to decocting. Solidifies stool while promoting urination — 'separates clear from turbid.'

indications: 1) Drains dampness, clears heat · 2) Urinary tract infections · 3) Dispels phlegm
boardPearl: Mineral-based. Must be placed in cheesecloth/gauze prior to decocting.

indications: 1) Jaundice and liver disorders · 2) Drains dampness, clears heat · 3) Resolves toxicity
boardPearl: Key herb for jaundice.

indications: 1) Edema · 2) Drains dampness, dispels wind · 3) Reduces inflammation, relieves pain
boardPearl: Unblocks channels. Treats edema and drains dampness. Reduces inflammation/relieves pain.

indications: 1) Best for pain in LOWER body (back & knees) · 2) Dispels Wind-Damp, relieves pain · 3) Shao Yin headache; toothache
contraindication: Very drying — can cause Yin harm
boardPearl: Lower body specialist. Shao Yin. Dui Yao with Qiang Huo — Du Huo moderate, Qiang Huo powerful.

indications: 1) Dispels Wind-Damp, alleviates pain/spasms · 2) Promotes circulation · 3) Softens small fish bones lodged in throat
boardPearl: Fish bones lodged in throat!

indications: 1) Relaxes sinews — esp. calf-muscle contractions · 2) Dispels Wind-Damp, alleviates pain · 3) Food stagnation
boardPearl: Very effective for relaxing sinews, especially calf-muscle contractions.

indications: 1) Nourishes blood, calms the womb/fetus · 2) Strengthens sinews and bones · 3) Promotes lactation; dry scaly skin
boardPearl: Nourishes blood and calms the womb. Promotes lactation. Benefits dry, scaly skin. From mulberry tree.

indications: 1) Weak or soft sinews/bones from chronic Wind-Cold-Damp with LV/KD deficiency · 2) Elderly and children · 3) Delayed ability to take first steps
boardPearl: Commonly used alone as herbal wine. Elderly. Children — delayed ability to take first steps.

indications: 1) Wind-Damp Bi — joint pain · 2) Jaundice · 3) Moistens intestines; counters drying of other herbs
boardPearl: Moistens intestines. Used to counter the drying nature of other herbs. Jaundice.

indications: 1) Cools and transforms Phlegm-Heat · 2) Releases the exterior · 3) Directs Qi downward
contraindication: Caution with cough from Yin deficiency
boardPearl: Both releases the exterior AND directs Qi downward.

indications: 1) Transforms phlegm, stops NON-productive cough · 2) Chronic cough, Yin-deficiency fire cough · 3) Clears heat, moistens lungs
boardPearl: Chronic and Yin-deficiency cough. Dui Yao with Xing Ren for dry cough with little/no phlegm.

indications: 1) Transforms phlegm, stops PRODUCTIVE cough (thick yellow phlegm) · 2) Clears acute Lung heat · 3) Moistens lungs
boardPearl: Stronger dispersing action — used more for externally contracted cough. Acute lung heat with thick yellow phlegm.

indications: 1) Transforms phlegm, stops cough · 2) Clears heat, resolves toxicity · 3) Breast abscess
contraindication: SP/ST deficiency; contra with Fu Zi
boardPearl: Stronger for clearing heat and transforming phlegm than Gua Lou Ren. Breast abscess.

indications: 1) Clears Phlegm-Heat · 2) Morning sickness · 3) Migraines
boardPearl: Goes to LU, ST, GB. Morning sickness. Migraines. Miner's disease.

indications: 1) Softens and transforms phlegm · 2) Reduces swelling, goiter · 3) Dissolves nodules
boardPearl: Kelp. Only herb in this category that does NOT go to the Lungs.

indications: 1) Softens and transforms phlegm · 2) Reduces swelling, promotes diuresis · 3) Clears heat, detoxifies
contraindication: SP/ST cold deficiency; contra with Gan Cao
boardPearl: Seaweed. Contra with Gan Cao (18 incompatibles).

indications: 1) Warms and transforms Cold-Phlegm · 2) Dries dampness · 3) Downbears Qi; stops vomiting
contraindication: Toxic in strong doses; contra in pregnancy
boardPearl: Very strong action of downbearing rebel Qi. Redirects Qi down and stops vomiting. Toxicity reduced by Sheng Jiang.

indications: 1) Dries dampness, expels phlegm · 2) Disperses Wind-Phlegm, stops spasm · 3) Facial paralysis
boardPearl: Facial paralysis. Disperses Wind-Phlegm and stops spasm.

indications: 1) Warms lungs, transforms phlegm · 2) Promotes flow of Qi · 3) Phlegm accumulation in chest from cold pattern
boardPearl: White mustard seed. Warms lungs and transforms phlegm.

indications: 1) Transforms phlegm, opens up lungs · 2) Directs Qi upward (guides other herbs to LU) · 3) Expels pus
boardPearl: Balloon flower root. Directs Qi upward — guides herbs to lungs. "Boat herb."

indications: 1) Phlegm accumulation in chest from cold · 2) Warms lungs, transforms phlegm · 3) Directs Qi downward
boardPearl: Redirects Qi down and stops vomiting (along with Zhi Ban Xia).

indications: 1) Calms lung, stops cough and wheezing · 2) Moistens intestines, alleviates constipation · 3) Various cough etiologies
boardPearl: Apricot seed. Metal. Dui Yao with Chuan Bei Mu for dry cough with little/no phlegm. Moistens intestines.

indications: 1) Stops coughing, resolves phlegm · 2) Treats asthma and lung conditions · 3) Various cough types
boardPearl: Dui Yao with Kuan Dong Hua.

indications: 1) Cough with profuse phlegm from cold binding LU · 2) Cough from LU Qi deficiency · 3) Moistens LU, directs Qi down, transforms phlegm
boardPearl: Flower. Best for cough with profuse phlegm from cold or LU Qi deficiency. Dui Yao with Zi Wan.

indications: 1) Best when exhale harder than inhale · 2) Directs Qi down, relieves wheezing · 3) Moistens intestines, unblocks bowels
boardPearl: Best to treat excess cough when exhale is more labored than inhale. Metal. Moistens intestines.

indications: 1) Moistens lung, stops cough from various etiologies · 2) Treats parasites (lice, pinworm) · 3) Chronic cough
boardPearl: Important herb for stopping coughs from various etiologies. Treats parasites — especially lice.

indications: 1) Cough due to heat (esp. yellow, copious sputum) · 2) Promotes urination, reduces edema · 3) Benefits eyes; treats hypertension
boardPearl: Sweet and cold. Drains heat from LU. Stops cough/wheeze. Promotes urination. Lowers blood pressure.

indications: 1) Strongly dries dampness · 2) Induces sweating, dispels Wind-Damp · 3) Improves vision (night blindness)
boardPearl: Dui Yao with Hou Po for damp encumbering spleen yang. Dui Yao with Huang Bai for damp-heat in lower jiao. Treats night blindness.

indications: 1) Promotes movement of Qi, relieves distention · 2) Moves and directs Qi downward · 3) Dries dampness, transforms phlegm; wheezing
boardPearl: Magnolia bark. Moves and directs Qi down. Dui Yao with Cang Zhu. Not for use during gestation.

indications: 1) Nausea, vomiting, morning sickness · 2) Regulates Qi, harmonizes MJ · 3) Gently releases exterior
boardPearl: Patchouli. Add near end of decocting — do not overcook. Gently releases exterior. Morning sickness.

indications: 1) Promotes Qi movement, transforms turbid damp · 2) Prevents Shu Di from stagnating · 3) Morning sickness; calms fetus
boardPearl: Calms fetus. Prevents Shu Di Huang from stagnating. Transforms turbid damp.

indications: 1) Warms MJ, promotes Qi movement · 2) Disseminates Lung Qi · 3) Abdominal pain, diarrhea, vomiting
boardPearl: Moves Qi more strongly than Cao Dou Kou. Also disseminates Lung Qi.

indications: 1) Strongly dries turbid dampness · 2) Warms MJ, transforms dampness · 3) Nausea, vomiting
boardPearl: Warmer and better at drying turbid damp than Bai Dou Kou. Strongly disperses cold, treats malarial disorders.

indications: 1) Promotes Qi movement, transforms dampness · 2) Malarial disorder · 3) Abdominal pain, diarrhea, vomiting
boardPearl: Malarial disorder. Dry fry with ginger for odor.

indications: 1) Relieves food stagnation from MEAT and greasy foods · 2) Transforms blood stasis · 3) Dissipates clumps
boardPearl: Hawthorn fruit. Charred for stopping diarrhea. Also transforms blood stasis and dissipates clumps.

indications: 1) Alcohol, fatty foods, grains, starches · 2) Promotes digestion, alleviates food stagnation · 3) Can reduce lactation
contraindication: Contains gluten; caution with nursing mothers
boardPearl: Fermented medicinal dough. Contains gluten. Can reduce lactation (30–120g).

indications: 1) Cold, starchy food stagnation · 2) Bloating and indigestion (also in infants) · 3) Stops lactation (30–120g)
boardPearl: Germinated barley. Can stop lactation at 30–120g.

indications: 1) Starchy food stagnation · 2) Dampness in digestive system · 3) Gentler action than Mai Ya
boardPearl: Germinated rice. Gentler than Mai Ya for starchy foods.

indications: 1) Food stagnation from greasy/rich foods · 2) Directs LU Qi down — cough and phlegm · 3) Recently used for hypertension
boardPearl: Radish seed. Also directs Lung Qi down for cough and phlegm. Recently used for hypertension.

indications: 1) Food stagnation from digestive energy deficiency · 2) Dissolves stones (kidney/gallbladder) · 3) Secures essence
boardPearl: Chicken gizzard lining. Dissolves stones. Secures essence.

indications: 1) Regulates Qi, improves digestion · 2) Dries dampness, transforms phlegm · 3) Prevents cloying herbs from creating stagnation
contraindication: Can cause vacuity of Qi and Yin
boardPearl: Dried tangerine peel. Warm, aromatic, improves SP transportation. Prevents cloying herbs from stagnating. Don't decoct >20 min. The older the peel, the better quality (aged tangerine peel is prized).

indications: 1) Most strongly regulates Qi of the LIVER · 2) Breaks up clumps · 3) Food stagnation
boardPearl: Green tangerine peel. Most strongly regulates LV Qi. Breaks up clumps.

indications: 1) Relieves distension/fullness in chest/abdomen · 2) Chest obstruction · 3) Promotes bowel movement; transforms phlegm
boardPearl: Immature bitter orange. Dui Yao with Zhi Ke. Chest obstruction. Transforms phlegm.

indications: 1) Liver Qi stagnation · 2) Promotes menstruation · 3) Moves Qi within blood through 12 channels
boardPearl: Essential gynecological herb. LV, GB, SJ. Spreads LV Qi in ribs, menses, and 12 channels.

indications: 1) Abdominal pain due to fullness · 2) Harmonizes intestines — diarrhea and tenesmus · 3) Strengthens Spleen, improves appetite
boardPearl: Warm, acrid, and bitter — harmonizes intestines to treat diarrhea and tenesmus. Strengthens SP.

indications: 1) Moves Qi, stops pain, warms KD · 2) Rebel Qi with pathogenic cold · 3) Urinary frequency/incontinence (warms KD); trunkal pain
boardPearl: Moves Qi, stops pain, warms KD to disperse cold. Urinary frequency/incontinence.

indications: 1) Warms KD — best for deficient cold in lower jiao · 2) KD failure to grasp Qi · 3) Regulates Qi, relieves pain
boardPearl: Agarwood. Best for deficient cold in lower jiao and KD failure to grasp Qi.

indications: 1) Moves LV Qi · 2) Clears Damp-Heat, kills parasites · 3) Guides heat out through urine
boardPearl: COLD nature (unusual for Regulate Qi). Clears Damp-Heat and kills parasites. Treats skin disease.

indications: 1) #1 for traumatic injury · 2) Stops bleeding WITHOUT causing stasis · 3) Stops pain, relieves swelling
boardPearl: Also known as Tian Qi. Stops bleeding without causing stasis. Topical powders/pastes.

indications: 1) Stops bleeding (nose/lungs) · 2) Astringent properties · 3) Uterine contraction; promotes urination
boardPearl: Uterine contraction. Tonifies SP, promotes urination.

indications: 1) Heavy menstrual bleeding · 2) Descends and retains hot blood (sourness) · 3) Topical for boils and sores
boardPearl: Descends and retains hot blood due to sourness.

indications: 1) Stops bleeding (LU, urinary tract) · 2) Promotes urination, cools blood · 3) Not cloying
boardPearl: Not cloying. Stops bleeding cough (with Ce Bai Ye).

indications: 1) Stops bleeding (bitter & astringent) · 2) Relieves diarrhea · 3) Kills parasites
boardPearl: Bitter and astringent — stops bleeding, relieves diarrhea, kills parasites.

indications: 1) Warms womb, calms the fetus (#1 in category) · 2) Menstrual disorders and bleeding · 3) Stops itching
boardPearl: Mugwort. Best choice in Stop Bleeding for calming the fetus. Warms womb. Stops itching.

indications: 1) #1 for bleeding cough · 2) Stops bleeding from wounds (topical) · 3) Promotes hair growth
boardPearl: #1 for bleeding cough. Promotes hair growth.

indications: 1) Stops bleeding (heavy menstrual, traumatic) · 2) Generates flesh · 3) Promotes blood circulation
boardPearl: Only herb in Stop Bleeding category that does NOT go to Liver. Generates flesh.

indications: 1) Moves Qi within blood below, expels wind above · 2) Headache (all types) · 3) Menstrual disorders, traumatic injuries
boardPearl: Moves Qi within blood below, expels wind above. #1 for headaches.

indications: 1) Invigorates blood, breaks stasis (Fire and Jue Yin) · 2) Calms irritability, calms spirit · 3) Gynecological; cardiovascular (angina)
boardPearl: Bitter, slightly cold — enters HT, PC, LV. Centered in chest, LV, PC, HT. Calms irritability.

indications: 1) Promotes blood circulation, removes stasis · 2) Cools heat, transforms stasis · 3) Liver disorders (sour → LV affinity)
boardPearl: Sour leads to liver affinity. Transforms stasis, cools heat, stops pain.

indications: 1) Invigorates blood, dissolves stasis · 2) Amenorrhea, dysmenorrhea · 3) Moistens intestines (downward)
boardPearl: Dui Yao with Hong Hua for amenorrhea and dysmenorrhea. Downward direction.

indications: 1) Unblocks menstruation · 2) Promotes blood circulation, removes stasis · 3) Skin disorders (lightweight)
boardPearl: Lightweight — treats skin disorders. Dui Yao with Tao Ren. Unblocks menstruation.

indications: 1) #1 analgesic — pain relief · 2) Invigorates blood · 3) Menstrual disorders
boardPearl: Known for analgesic properties. #1 for pain relief in this category.

indications: 1) Invigorates blood, cools blood · 2) Jaundice · 3) Liver problems, traumatic injuries
boardPearl: Acrid, bitter, cold. Cools blood. Jaundice.

indications: 1) Breaks blood stasis for gynecological issues · 2) Regulates menstruation · 3) Postpartum abdominal pain
boardPearl: Moves blood WITHOUT further depleting blood (along with Ze Lan).

indications: 1) Back pain, traumatic injuries · 2) Menstrual disorders · 3) Tonifies KD; strengthens sinews/bones (prepared)
boardPearl: Unprepared = breaks blood. Prepared = tonifies LV/KD, strengthens sinews and bones.

indications: 1) Rescues devastated Yang · 2) Tonifies fire at gate of vitality (KD Yang) · 3) Disperses cold, warms channels, alleviates pain
contraindication: TOXIC — contra in pregnancy, Yin deficiency, excess Yang
boardPearl: Pure Yang. Fast, penetrating — goes to ALL channels. Rescues devastated Yang. Aids HT yang above and KD yang below. Antidote: Lu Dou. 18 Incompatibilities: incompatible with Ban Xia, Gua Lou, Bei Mu, Bai Lian, Bai Ji.

indications: 1) Warms Middle Jiao, expels cold · 2) Rescues Yang (with Zhi Fu Zi) · 3) Warms LU, transforms thin mucus; stops bleeding
boardPearl: Dried ginger. Less dispersing than Sheng Jiang, more effective at warming middle. Longer-lasting; best for Yang deficiency cold of SP/ST.

indications: 1) Leads fire back to source (true cold, false heat) · 2) Fortifies Yang, warms KD Yang · 3) Disperses deep cold; cold in uterus; Bi joint pain
boardPearl: Cinnamon bark. Leads fire back to source — principle herb for TRUE COLD, FALSE HEAT. More strongly warms KD Yang than Zhi Fu Zi.

indications: 1) Warms MJ, disperses cold, moves Qi, stops pain · 2) Stops vomiting due to cold · 3) Redirects rebellious Qi downward; leads fire downward
boardPearl: Stops vomiting due to cold. Warms SP, stops diarrhea. Leads fire downwards.

indications: 1) Disperses cold, warms LV/KD, stops pain · 2) Regulates Qi, harmonizes ST · 3) Bulging disorders
boardPearl: Fennel. Disperses cold, warms LV and KD, stops pain. Regulates Qi in LV channel — bulging disorders.

indications: 1) Warms MJ/LJ, improves digestion · 2) Directs Qi downward to stop hiccup · 3) Assists KD Yang (impotence)
contraindication: Yin deficiency with heat; contra with Yu Jin
boardPearl: Cloves. Directs Qi downward — stops hiccup. Assists KD Yang for impotence.

indications: 1) Tonifies PRIMAL Qi (most potent) · 2) Generates fluids, calms spirit · 3) Revives collapse; generates and controls blood
contraindication: Excess heat; contra with Li Lu
boardPearl: VERY FAST-ACTING. Most potent Qi tonic. Only herb that tonifies Primal Qi. Generates fluids directly.

indications: 1) Tonifies Qi (SP/LU focused) · 2) Fatigue, poor digestion, chronic diarrhea · 3) Affordable alternative to Ren Shen
boardPearl: Does NOT directly generate fluids like Ren Shen. More focused on SP/LU. Affordable alternative.

indications: 1) Tonifies Qi weakly · 2) Nourishes Yin, generates fluid · 3) Fatigue, shortness of breath, poor appetite
boardPearl: Weak Qi tonic — for mild deficiency. Also nourishes Yin.

indications: 1) Tonifies Qi (SP/LU), raises Yang · 2) Stabilizes exterior, stops sweat · 3) Facilitates urination; promotes healing
contraindication: Excess exterior conditions; Yin deficiency with heat
boardPearl: Raises Yang. Stabilizes exterior (with Bai Zhu). Stops sweat. Facilitates urination.

indications: 1) Tonifies SP Qi · 2) Dries damp · 3) Calms fetus
boardPearl: Dries damp. Calms fetus. Stabilizes exterior with Huang Qi.

indications: 1) Universal envoy — harmonizes, moderates toxicity · 2) Moistens lungs, stops cough · 3) Moderates spasms; unprepared drains fire, prepared tonifies SP Qi
contraindication: Excess damp; contra with Hai Zao, Da Ji, Yuan Hua, Gan Sui
boardPearl: Enters ALL 12 channels. Universal envoy. Unprepared = drains fire. Prepared (honey-fried) = tonifies SP Qi. Chief in palpitations formula.

indications: 1) Tonifies Qi and nourishes Yin (SP/LU) · 2) Diarrhea, frequent urination · 3) Mild astringent
1) #1 herb for tonifying and invigorating blood · 2) Regulates menstruation, alleviates pain · 3) Moistens intestines, unblocks bowels
contraindication: Caution with diarrhea from SP deficiency; excess dampness
boardPearl: Head (Gui Tou) = stops bleeding; Body (Gui Shen) = tonifies blood; Tail (Gui Wei) = invigorates/breaks blood. Whole herb = harmonizes blood. Key herb in Si Wu Tang. Dui Yao with Huang Qi (Dang Gui Bu Xue Tang) — tonifies Qi to generate blood.

indications: 1) Tonifies blood (key herb) · 2) Nourishes Yin, fills essence (Jing) · 3) Treats LV/KD deficiency — dizziness, tinnitus, premature graying
contraindication: Very cloying — can cause bloating, loose stools, poor appetite
boardPearl: Extremely cloying — combine with Qi-movers like Sha Ren or Chen Pi to prevent stagnation. Key herb in Si Wu Tang and Liu Wei Di Huang Wan. Prepared from Sheng Di Huang via wine-steaming.

indications: 1) Nourishes blood, regulates menstruation · 2) Calms LV Yang, alleviates pain (flanks, abdomen, limbs) · 3) Preserves Yin, adjusts Ying and Wei; stops sweating
contraindication: Yang deficiency cold; contra with Li Lu (19 Fears)
boardPearl: Restrains Yin — astringes Yin to stop sweating. Softens the LV. Dui Yao with Chai Hu for LV Qi stagnation (one spreads, one restrains). Dui Yao with Gui Zhi for harmonizing Ying/Wei. Sour + Gan Cao (sweet) = relaxes spasms.

indications: 1) Tonifies LV/KD, nourishes blood/essence — premature graying, dizziness · 2) Treats malaria (unprepared) · 3) Moistens intestines (unprepared); secures essence
contraindication: SP deficiency with damp or diarrhea; hepatotoxicity risk with prolonged use
boardPearl: PREPARED (Zhi He Shou Wu) = tonifies LV/KD, nourishes blood, darkens hair. UNPREPARED (Sheng He Shou Wu) = treats malaria, moistens intestines, resolves toxicity. Two completely different uses based on preparation!

indications: 1) Tonifies blood, stops bleeding · 2) Nourishes Yin, moistens dryness (esp. LU) · 3) Calms fetus for bleeding during pregnancy
contraindication: Very cloying — SP deficiency with damp; impairs digestion
boardPearl: Donkey-hide gelatin. Must be dissolved separately (melted in strained decoction, not boiled with herbs). Stops bleeding and nourishes blood simultaneously. Key for Yin-deficiency dry cough.

indications: 1) Tonifies HT and SP blood · 2) Calms the spirit — insomnia, palpitations, poor memory · 3) Nourishes blood for pensiveness and worry
boardPearl: Longan fruit. Key herb in Gui Pi Tang for HT/SP blood deficiency with insomnia and anxiety. Sweet and warm — easily digested.

indications: 1) Tonifies KD Yang, augments essence and blood · 2) Strengthens sinews and bones — developmental delays in children · 3) Regulates Chong and Ren Mai — infertility, impotence
contraindication: Yin deficiency with heat signs; excess heat; hypertension
boardPearl: Deer antler velvet. Tonifies Du Mai. Most powerful to tonify KD Yang and essence (Jing). Start with small dose, increase gradually. Grind to powder — do not decoct.

indications: 1) Tonifies KD Yang — impotence, infertility, frequent urination · 2) Dispels Wind-Cold-Damp Bi · 3) Strengthens sinews and bones
boardPearl: "Horny goat weed" — legend says goats eating it became sexually hyperactive. Warms KD, strengthens Yang, AND treats Bi syndrome. Dual action herb.

indications: 1) Tonifies LV/KD — lower back pain, knee weakness · 2) Strengthens sinews and bones · 3) Calms the fetus — prevents miscarriage
boardPearl: Key herb for lower back pain from KD deficiency. Calms fetus. Also lowers blood pressure. Bark has characteristic rubber-like threads when broken. Dui Yao with Xu Duan for back pain and restless fetus.

indications: 1) Tonifies LV/KD — lower back, knee pain · 2) Mends sinews and bones — traumatic injury, fractures · 3) Calms fetus; stops uterine bleeding
boardPearl: Name literally means "restore what is broken." Promotes blood circulation to mend bones. Dui Yao with Du Zhong for back pain and restless fetus. Also invigorates blood to promote healing of fractures.

indications: 1) Tonifies KD Yang — impotence, infertility, frequent urination · 2) Strengthens sinews and bones · 3) Dispels Wind-Cold-Damp (Bi syndrome)
boardPearl: Warms KD without being overly drying. Good for patients who need KD Yang support but can't tolerate excessively hot herbs.

indications: 1) Tonifies KD Yang, secures essence — impotence, enuresis, incontinence · 2) Warms SP Yang — "5th watch" (cock-crow) diarrhea · 3) Assists KD to grasp LU Qi — wheezing
boardPearl: Classic herb for dawn/cock-crow diarrhea (SP/KD Yang deficiency). Dui Yao with Rou Dou Kou in Si Shen Wan for dawn diarrhea. Also used topically for vitiligo.

indications: 1) Tonifies KD Yang AND LU Yin simultaneously · 2) Augments essence, strengthens Jing · 3) Transforms phlegm, stops cough and bleeding
boardPearl: "Winter worm, summer grass" — caterpillar fungus. Unique: tonifies BOTH KD Yang and LU Yin without being cloying or drying. Gentle, balanced tonic suitable for chronic deficiency. Very expensive.

indications: 1) Tonifies KD/LV — secures essence, stops enuresis · 2) Benefits eyes — blurred vision from LV/KD deficiency · 3) Calms fetus; prevents miscarriage
contraindication: Yin deficiency with fire; constipation; dark scanty urine
boardPearl: Dodder seed. Tonifies without being cloying. Nourishes Yin AND Yang — balanced tonic. Also strengthens SP to stop diarrhea. Must be placed in cheesecloth/gauze prior to decocting.

indications: 1) Tonifies LV/KD — augments essence (Jing) and blood · 2) Tonifies KD Yang and LU Qi simultaneously · 3) Chronic cough/wheezing from LU/KD deficiency
contraindication: Yin deficiency with heat; pathogenic excess
boardPearl: Human placenta. Considered the BEST substance to tonify Jing (essence). Tonifies Qi, blood, and essence simultaneously — addresses all aspects of deficiency. Rare/expensive; ethical considerations.

indications: 1) Tonifies LU Qi and KD Yang · 2) Assists KD to grasp Qi — chronic asthma/wheezing · 3) Augments essence, tonifies blood
boardPearl: Gecko lizard. Excellent for chronic wheezing/dyspnea from KD failing to grasp LU Qi. Augments essence for impotence. Often ground to powder and taken in wine.

indications: 1) Nourishes LU Yin, clears LU heat · 2) Nourishes ST Yin, generates fluids · 3) Dry cough with little sputum
contraindication: Wind-Cold cough; contra with Li Lu
boardPearl: Bei Sha Shen vs Nan Sha Shen: Bei = better for nourishing Yin (more moistening); Nan = also expels phlegm (more dispersing). Bei enters LU/ST; Nan enters LU/ST. Bei is from Glehniae; Nan from Adenophorae.

indications: 1) Nourishes LU Yin, clears LU heat · 2) Expels phlegm (unlike Bei Sha Shen) · 3) Tonifies SP Qi
contraindication: Wind-Cold cough; contra with Li Lu
boardPearl: Similar to Bei Sha Shen but with added ability to expel phlegm and mildly tonify Qi. Bei = pure Yin nourishing; Nan = Yin nourishing + phlegm-expelling + Qi-tonifying.

indications: 1) Moistens LU, nourishes Yin — dry cough, hemoptysis · 2) Nourishes ST Yin, generates fluids · 3) Clears HT, eliminates irritability — insomnia
contraindication: SP deficiency with damp; diarrhea; wind-cold cough
boardPearl: Dui Yao with Tian Men Dong for LU Yin deficiency. Mai Men Dong is gentler and also nourishes HT/ST. In Sheng Mai San with Ren Shen + Wu Wei Zi.

indications: 1) Nourishes KD Yin, clears LU heat · 2) Moistens LU, generates fluids — dry cough from Yin deficiency · 3) Generates fluids, moistens intestines
contraindication: SP deficiency with damp; diarrhea; wind-cold cough
boardPearl: Stronger and colder than Mai Men Dong. Goes deeper — enters KD (Mai Men Dong enters HT). Better for KD Yin deficiency with LU involvement. Mai Men Dong = HT/LU/ST; Tian Men Dong = KD/LU.

indications: 1) Nourishes ST Yin, generates fluids — thirst, dry mouth · 2) Nourishes KD Yin — lower back pain, weakness · 3) Brightens eyes, strengthens lower back
contraindication: Damp-warmth not yet resolved; early-stage febrile disease
boardPearl: Orchid stem. Especially effective for nourishing ST Yin and generating fluids. Also brightens eyes from KD/LV Yin deficiency.

indications: 1) Moistens LU, nourishes ST — dry cough, dry throat · 2) Generates fluids, extinguishes wind — dizziness, irritability · 3) Nourishes Yin for externally-contracted Wind-Heat with Yin deficiency
boardPearl: Solomon's seal. Gentle Yin tonic — can be used even when exterior pathogen is present (unlike most Yin tonics). Nourishes without cloying.

indications: 1) Nourishes LV/KD — dizziness, blurred vision, premature graying · 2) Brightens eyes · 3) Moistens LU — consumptive cough
contraindication: SP deficiency with damp; excess heat
boardPearl: Wolfberry / goji berry. Key herb in Qi Ju Di Huang Wan for eyes. Nourishes LV blood AND KD Yin — dual action. Gentle, commonly used as food therapy.

indications: 1) Nourishes LV/KD Yin — dizziness, tinnitus, premature graying · 2) Clears deficiency heat · 3) Brightens eyes
contraindication: SP deficiency with damp; Yang deficiency diarrhea
boardPearl: Privet fruit. Dui Yao with Mo Han Lian (Han Lian Cao) in Er Zhi Wan — together nourish LV/KD Yin. Gentle Yin tonic — not cloying. Also darkens hair.

indications: 1) Nourishes LV/KD Yin · 2) Cools blood, stops bleeding — hematemesis, epistaxis, hematuria · 3) Premature graying of hair
contraindication: SP deficiency with damp; Yang deficiency
boardPearl: Eclipta. Dui Yao with Nu Zhen Zi in Er Zhi Wan. Unique in Tonify Yin category: also stops bleeding (cools blood). Darkens hair.

indications: 1) Nourishes Yin, anchors Yang — dizziness, tinnitus · 2) Benefits KD, strengthens bones — developmental delays · 3) Nourishes blood, enriches HT — palpitations, insomnia
boardPearl: Tortoise plastron (ventral shell). Very heavy — anchors/settles. Strengthens bones in children with delayed development. Dui Yao with Bie Jia — Gui Ban is more for nourishing blood/anchoring Yang; Bie Jia is more for nourishing Yin/clearing deficiency heat. Must be decocted first (30+ min).

indications: 1) Nourishes Yin, subdues Yang — steaming bone disorder, night sweats · 2) Softens hardness, dissipates nodules — hepatosplenomegaly · 3) Invigorates blood
boardPearl: Soft-shelled turtle shell (dorsal). Key for steaming bone disorder in Qing Gu San. Softens hardness for masses and nodules (hepatosplenomegaly). Bie Jia vs Gui Ban: Bie Jia = clears deficiency heat + softens hardness; Gui Ban = anchors Yang + strengthens bones. Must be decocted first (30+ min).

indications: 1) Contains leakage of LU Qi — chronic cough, wheezing · 2) Tonifies KD, binds essence — spermatorrhea, urinary incontinence · 3) Generates fluids, stops sweating — spontaneous/night sweating
boardPearl: "Five Flavor Berry" — sour (primary), sweet, bitter, acrid, salty. In Sheng Mai San with Ren Shen + Mai Men Dong. Inhibits sweating, secures essence, stops diarrhea, calms spirit. Enters all three treasures. Also treats insomnia and palpitations.

indications: 1) Stabilizes KD, secures essence — spermatorrhea, enuresis · 2) Stops excessive sweating from devastated Yang/Yin · 3) Tonifies LV/KD — dizziness, tinnitus, lower back pain
boardPearl: Key herb in Liu Wei Di Huang Wan. Does NOT bind the intestines — important distinction! Can be used for sweating from devastated Yang (with Ren Shen, Zhi Fu Zi). Stabilizes but also mildly tonifies.

indications: 1) Expels roundworms — abdominal pain, vomiting (Wu Mei Wan) · 2) Generates fluids — thirst, dry mouth · 3) Stops chronic cough; stops chronic diarrhea; stops bleeding
boardPearl: Smoked plum. #1 for roundworms — roundworms "like sweet, avoid sour." Wu Mei Wan combines sour, bitter, acrid, sweet for parasites. Also generates fluids for deficiency thirst.

indications: 1) Controls acidity — epigastric pain, acid reflux · 2) Stops bleeding — uterine/traumatic bleeding · 3) Secures essence, stops vaginal discharge
boardPearl: Cuttlefish bone. Controls acidity (like a natural antacid). Also used topically for non-healing ulcers/sores. Dui Yao with Qian Cao for uterine bleeding.

indications: 1) Tonifies SP, stops diarrhea · 2) Tonifies KD, secures essence — spermatorrhea, vaginal discharge · 3) Nourishes HT, calms spirit — insomnia, palpitations
boardPearl: Lotus seed. Enters HT, SP, KD — addresses all three. The plumule (Lian Zi Xin) is separately used to drain HT fire and is BITTER and COLD.

indications: 1) Strengthens SP, stops diarrhea · 2) Secures KD essence — spermatorrhea, enuresis · 3) Expels dampness, stops vaginal discharge
boardPearl: Fox nut / Euryale seed. Similar to Lian Zi but stronger at expelling dampness and does NOT calm the spirit (no HT action). Dui Yao with Lian Zi for SP/KD deficiency.

indications: 1) Inhibits sweating — spontaneous and night sweating · 2) Nourishes HT Qi · 3) Clears heat from deficiency
boardPearl: Light wheat (floats in water). Key herb for stopping sweating. In Gan Mai Da Zao Tang (with Gan Cao + Da Zao) for restless organ disorder (Zang Zao).

indications: 1) Warms MJ, binds intestines — chronic diarrhea · 2) Moves Qi, warms SP Yang · 3) Dawn/cock-crow diarrhea (with Bu Gu Zhi)
boardPearl: Nutmeg. Dui Yao with Bu Gu Zhi in Si Shen Wan for dawn (cock-crow) diarrhea from SP/KD Yang deficiency. Binds intestines more effectively when baked/roasted.

indications: 1) Astringes LU — stops cough and wheezing · 2) Eliminates dampness — stops vaginal discharge · 3) Stabilizes lower jiao — stops urinary incontinence
contraindication: SLIGHTLY TOXIC — limit dose; avoid raw/excess
boardPearl: Ginkgo nut. Slightly toxic — must be cooked. Stops cough, stops wheezing, stops discharge, stops incontinence — multi-astringing. Cook before use; raw seeds are more toxic.

indications: 1) Secures essence — spermatorrhea, enuresis · 2) Stops urinary incontinence · 3) Binds intestines — chronic diarrhea; stops vaginal discharge
boardPearl: Cherokee rosehip. Dui Yao with Qian Shi in Shui Lu Er Xian Dan — together secure essence and stop discharge.

indications: 1) Clears heat, dries damp — chronic diarrhea/dysentery · 2) Stops bleeding — uterine bleeding, hemorrhoids · 3) Stops vaginal discharge; kills parasites
boardPearl: Tree of Heaven bark. Cold and bitter — used for damp-heat patterns (unlike most astringents which are warm). Dual action: binds AND clears damp-heat.

indications: 1) Anchors and calms spirit — anxiety, palpitations, insomnia · 2) Calms LV, anchors floating Yang — irritability, dizziness · 3) Prevents leakage — sweating, spermatorrhea, vaginal discharge; generates flesh
boardPearl: Fossilized bone. Dui Yao with Mu Li — together anchor Yang, calm spirit, prevent leakage. Long Gu is better for calming spirit; Mu Li is better for softening hardness. Must be decocted first (30+ min). Calcined = stronger astringent.

indications: 1) Calms spirit, anchors floating Yang — irritability, insomnia · 2) Softens hardness, dissipates nodules — scrofula, goiter · 3) Absorbs acidity — epigastric pain; prevents leakage
contraindication: Not for prolonged use in excess
boardPearl: Oyster shell. Dui Yao with Long Gu. Unique: SOFTENS HARDNESS and dissipates nodules (Long Gu cannot). Also absorbs acidity (like Hai Piao Xiao). Must be decocted first (30+ min). Calcined = stronger astringent + antacid.

indications: 1) Anchors and calms spirit — palpitations, tremors, insomnia · 2) Nourishes KD, augments LV — benefits eyes and ears · 3) Aids KD in grasping Qi — asthma from KD deficiency
contraindication: SP/ST deficiency; excess use damages Qi
boardPearl: Magnetite. Heavy mineral — settles and anchors. Benefits both eyes AND ears (through KD/LV connection). Must be decocted first (30+ min) and crushed.

indications: 1) #1 herb for insomnia — nourishes HT, calms spirit · 2) Nourishes LV blood — irritability, dizziness · 3) Prevents abnormal sweating
boardPearl: #1 insomnia herb. Key herb in Suan Zao Ren Tang. Nourishes HT Yin/blood to calm spirit. Works for BOTH deficiency insomnia (main use) and excess insomnia. Raw = calms; dry-fried = more commonly used for insomnia.

indications: 1) Calms spirit — insomnia, palpitations, anxiety · 2) Expels phlegm, clears orifices — seizures, mental confusion · 3) Dissipates swellings, reduces abscesses
contraindication: Gastritis/peptic ulcer (irritates stomach); use with Gan Cao to mitigate
boardPearl: Expels phlegm from HT orifices — key for phlegm misting the HT causing confusion/seizures. Facilitates HT-KD communication. May irritate the stomach — combine with Gan Cao or Da Zao.

indications: 1) Nourishes HT, calms spirit — insomnia, palpitations · 2) Moistens intestines, unblocks bowels — elderly/postpartum constipation · 3) Night sweats from Yin deficiency
boardPearl: Arborvitae seed. Nourishes HT blood to calm spirit AND moistens intestines — dual action. Gentler than Suan Zao Ren. Good for elderly patients with insomnia + constipation.

indications: 1) Calms spirit — anxiety, palpitations, insomnia, seizures · 2) Invigorates blood, dissipates stasis — amenorrhea · 3) Promotes urination, unblocks Lin syndrome
contraindication: Yin deficiency without blood stasis; pregnancy
boardPearl: Amber (fossilized tree resin). Unique: calms spirit AND invigorates blood AND promotes urination — triple action. Grind to powder, do not decoct.

indications: 1) Nourishes HT, calms spirit — insomnia, dream-disturbed sleep · 2) Nourishes blood — generalized pain/itching from blood deficiency · 3) Unblocks channels/collaterals
boardPearl: Stem of He Shou Wu plant (Caulis = stem). Nourishes blood to calm spirit. Also used externally as wash for itching/rashes. Gentle — good for mild insomnia.

indications: 1) Calms spirit — depression, irritability, insomnia · 2) Invigorates blood, alleviates pain — traumatic injuries, fractures · 3) Dissipates swellings, generates flesh
boardPearl: "Collective happiness bark" — Mimosa tree bark. Used for emotional constraint, depression, and grief. Calms spirit AND invigorates blood — dual action for emotional and physical trauma.

indications: 1) Extinguishes LV wind — dizziness, headache, seizures, childhood convulsions · 2) Calms LV Yang rising — hypertension, migraines · 3) Dispels Wind-Damp Bi — numbness in extremities
contraindication: Blood deficiency (can worsen dryness); caution with Yin deficiency
boardPearl: Treats ALL forms of internal wind. Dui Yao with Gou Teng for LV Yang rising and LV wind. Sweet, neutral — gentle, moistening. Used in Tian Ma Gou Teng Yin for hypertension headache. Key herb for dizziness from any cause involving wind.

indications: 1) Extinguishes wind, alleviates spasm — childhood convulsions, eclampsia · 2) Drains LV heat, pacifies LV Yang · 3) Releases exterior wind — headache, dizziness
contraindication: Do NOT overcook — add in last 5 minutes
boardPearl: Uncaria hooks. Treats BOTH internal AND external wind. Best for childhood convulsions (febrile seizures). Dui Yao with Tian Ma. Must add near end of decoction (last 5 min) — active compounds destroyed by prolonged heat.

indications: 1) Extinguishes wind, controls spasms/tremors — high fever with convulsions · 2) Calms LV, anchors Yang — extreme headache, dizziness · 3) Drains LV heat, clears eyes — red eyes from LV fire
contraindication: SP deficiency cold; wind from blood deficiency
boardPearl: Antelope horn (saiga). Strongest in category for extreme heat generating wind (high fever + convulsions). Very cold — reserved for severe excess heat conditions. Endangered species — substitutes often used. Must be decocted separately for 2+ hours or ground to powder.

indications: 1) Extinguishes wind, stops tremors/spasms — all types of seizures · 2) Attacks and resolves fire toxin — sores, nodules · 3) Unblocks collaterals, stops pain — stubborn Bi, headache
contraindication: TOXIC — strict dosing; pregnancy; blood deficiency generating wind
boardPearl: Scorpion. Toxic — use with care. Dui Yao with Wu Gong (centipede) for severe convulsions. Ground to powder, often taken directly (not decocted). Treats stubborn headache and Bi syndrome.

indications: 1) Extinguishes wind — STRONGEST for convulsions and spasms · 2) Attacks toxin, dissipates nodules — snake bites, sores · 3) Unblocks collaterals, stops pain — stubborn Bi, headache
boardPearl: Centipede. Strongest insect in category for convulsions/spasms. More powerful but more toxic than Quan Xie. Dui Yao with Quan Xie. Good for tetanus and lockjaw. Often ground to powder.

indications: 1) Clears heat, extinguishes wind — high fever with convulsions · 2) Clears heat, unblocks channels — Bi syndrome from heat · 3) Promotes urination; clears LU heat — asthma/wheezing
boardPearl: Earthworm. Cold nature — best for wind from HEAT (unlike warm insects like Wu Gong). Also clears LU heat for wheezing AND promotes urination. Unique triple action: extinguishes wind + treats asthma + promotes urination.

indications: 1) Extinguishes wind, stops spasms — childhood convulsions, facial paralysis · 2) Dispels wind, disperses Wind-Heat — sore throat, hoarseness · 3) Transforms phlegm, dissipates nodules — scrofula
boardPearl: Silkworm (killed by Beauveria fungus). Key for wind-phlegm patterns — facial paralysis (Bell's palsy), headache from wind-phlegm. Also treats sore throat (Wind-Heat). Transforms phlegm AND extinguishes wind — dual action.

indications: 1) Calms LV, anchors floating Yang — dizziness, tinnitus · 2) Strongly descends rebellious Qi — belching, hiccup, vomiting, wheezing · 3) Cools blood, stops bleeding
boardPearl: Iron oxide mineral (hematite). Heavy — powerfully descends rebellious Qi and anchors Yang. Dui Yao with Xuan Fu Hua (Xuan Fu Dai Zhe Tang) for rebellious ST Qi with belching/vomiting. Must be decocted first (30+ min) and crushed.

indications: 1) Calms LV, anchors Yang — headache, dizziness from LV Yang rising · 2) Disperses wind, brightens eyes — red, itchy, teary eyes · 3) Dispels wind, stops itching — skin rashes, vitiligo
boardPearl: Puncture vine / tribulus. Enters LV channel exclusively. Treats eyes (wind-heat), headache, AND itching — triple action involving LV wind. Calms LV Yang while also dispersing external wind.
